title: Old Stone Chimney
content:
В В В В The rising moon on the peaks was blending
В В В В Her silver light with the sunset glow,
В В В В When a swagman came as the day was ending
В В В В Along a path that he seemed to know.
В В В В But all the fences were gone or going, 
В В В В The hand of ruin was everywhere;
В В В В The creek unchecked in its course was flowing,
В В В В For none of the old clay dam was there.

В В В В Here Time had been with his swiftest changes,
В В В В And husbandry had westward flown;
В В В В The cattle tracks in the rugged ranges
В В В В Were long ago with the scrub oпїЅergrown.
В В В В It must have needed long years to soften
В В В В The road, that as hard as rock had been;
В В В В The mountain path he had trod so often
В В В В Lay hidden now with a carpet green.

В В В В He thought at times from the mountain courses
В В В В He heard the sound of a bullock bell,
В В В В The distant gallop of stockmenпїЅs horses,
В В В В The stockwhipпїЅs crack that he knew so well:
В В В В But these were sounds of his memory only,
В В В В And they were gone from the flat and hill,
В В В В For when he listened the place was lonely,
В В В В The range was dumb and the bush was still.

В В В В The swagman paused by the gap and faltered,
В В В В For down the gully he feared to go,
В В В В The scene in memory never altered, 
В В В В The scene before him had altered so.
В В В В But hope is strong, and his heart grew bolder,
В В В В And over his sorrows he raised his head,
В В В В He turned his swag to the other shoulder,
В В В В And plodded on with a firmer tread.

В В В В Ah, hope is always the keenest hearer,
В В В В And fancies much when assailed by fear;
В В В В The swagman thought, as the farm drew nearer,
В В В В He heard the sounds that he used to hear.
В В В В His weary heart for a moment bounded,
В В В В For a moment brief he forgot his dread;
В В В В For plainly still in his memory sounded
В В В В The welcome bark of a dog long dead.

В В В В A few steps more and his face grew ghostly,
В В В В Then white as death in the twilight grey;
В В В В Deserted wholly, and ruined mostly,
В В В В The Old Selection before him lay.
В В В В Like startled spectres that paused and listened,
В В В В The few white posts of the stockyard stood;
В В В В And seemed to move as the moonlight glistened
В В В В And paled again on the whitened wood.

В В В В And thus he came, from a life long banished
В В В В To other lands, and of peace bereft,
В В В В To find the farm and the homestead vanished,
В В В В And only the old stone chimney left.
В В В В The field his father had cleared and gardened
В В В В Was overgrown with saplings now;
В В В В The rain had set and the drought had hardened
В В В В The furrows made by a vanished plough.

В В В В And this, and this was the longed-for haven
В В В В Where he might rest from a life of woe;
В В В В He read a name on the mantel graven, 
В В В В The name was his ere he stained it so.
В В В В пїЅAnd so remorse on my care encroaches, 
В В В В пїЅI have not suffered enough,пїЅ he said;
В В В В пїЅThat name is pregnant with deep reproaches, 
В В В В пїЅThe past wonпїЅt bury dishonoured dead!пїЅ

В В В В Ah, now he knew it was long years after,
В В В В And felt how swiftly a long year speeds;
В В В В The hardwood post and the beam and rafter
В В В В Had rotted long in the tangled weeds.
В В В В He found that time had for years been sowing
В В В В The coarse wild scrub on the homestead path,
В В В В And saw young trees by the chimney growing,
В В В В And mountain ferns on the wide stone hearth.

В В В В He wildly thought of the evil courses
В В В В That brought disgrace on his fatherпїЅs name;
В В В В The escort robbed, and the stolen horses,
В В В В The felonпїЅs dock with its lasting shame.
В В В В пїЅAh, God! Ah, God! is there then no pardon?пїЅ
В В В В He cried in a voice that was strained and hoarse;
В В В В He fell on the weeds that were once a garden,
В В В В And sobbed aloud in his great remorse.

В В В В But grief must end, and his heart ceased aching
В В В В When pitying sleep to his eye-lids crept,
В В В В And home and friends who were lost in waking,
В В В В They all came back while the stockman slept.
В В В В And when he woke on the empty morrow,
В В В В The pain at his heart was a deadened pain;
В В В В And bravely bearing his load of sorrow,
В В В В He wandered back to the world again.
meaning:
"Old Stone Chimney" is a quintessential example of Henry Lawson's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
